name: Atoms
subject: Physics

sections.heading: 1. Thomson's Plum Pudding Model
sections.body:
J.J. Thomson (1898) proposed that an atom is a sphere of positive charge in which electrons are embedded like plums in a pudding. The atom is electrically neutral — total positive charge equals total negative.

The model could explain neutrality and approximate size but failed to account for line spectra, stability of atoms, or — fatally — the results of Rutherford's scattering experiment.

sections.heading: 2. Rutherford's Alpha-Scattering Experiment and Nuclear Model
sections.body:
Geiger and Marsden (under Rutherford, 1911) bombarded a thin gold foil with $\alpha$-particles. Most $\alpha$s passed through with little or no deflection, but a small fraction (~1 in 8000) was scattered by more than 90° — some bouncing nearly straight back.

Conclusions:

  • Most of the atom is empty space.
  • Almost all the mass and the entire positive charge is concentrated in a tiny central nucleus ($r \sim 10^{-15}$ m vs atom $\sim 10^{-10}$ m).
  • Electrons orbit the nucleus at distance.

Distance of closest approach for head-on collision: $\alpha$-particle KE converts entirely to potential energy: $$r_0 = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\dfrac{2Ze \cdot e}{K}$$

Drawback: classical electrodynamics predicts orbiting electrons should radiate continuously and spiral into nucleus in $\sim 10^{-8}$ s — atoms would be unstable.

sections.heading: 3. Atomic Spectra
sections.body:
Excited atoms emit light only at certain discrete wavelengths — a line spectrum, characteristic of the element. Each atom has its 'fingerprint'.

For hydrogen, wavelengths are given by the Rydberg formula: $$\dfrac{1}{\lambda} = R\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n_1^2} - \dfrac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$$ where $n_2 > n_1$, $R = 1.097 \times 10^7$ m⁻¹ is the Rydberg constant.

Series:

  • Lyman ($n_1 = 1$): UV.
  • Balmer ($n_1 = 2$): visible (Hα 656 nm, Hβ 486 nm, etc.).
  • Paschen ($n_1 = 3$): IR.
  • Brackett ($n_1 = 4$), Pfund ($n_1 = 5$): far IR.

sections.heading: 4. Bohr's Postulates
sections.body:
Bohr (1913) modified Rutherford's model with quantum postulates:

  1. Stationary orbits: Electrons revolve only in certain allowed orbits without radiating energy.
  2. Quantisation of angular momentum: $$L = mvr = \dfrac{nh}{2\pi}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ where $n$ is the principal quantum number.
  3. Frequency condition: When an electron jumps from a higher orbit ($n_2$) to a lower one ($n_1$), it emits a photon: $$h\nu = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1}$$ Conversely, absorption causes upward jump.

sections.heading: 5. Bohr Model of Hydrogen — Radii and Energies
sections.body:
Setting Coulomb force = centripetal force, plus Bohr's quantisation, for hydrogen-like ($Z$ = atomic number, single electron) atom:

Radius of $n$-th orbit: $$r_n = \dfrac{n^2 h^2 \varepsilon_0}{\pi m e^2 Z} = \dfrac{n^2}{Z}\,a_0$$ where $a_0 = 0.529$ Å is the Bohr radius (for H with $n = 1$).

Speed: $v_n = Ze^2/(2\varepsilon_0 nh)$, decreases as $1/n$.

Energy: $$E_n = -\dfrac{m e^4 Z^2}{8\varepsilon_0^2 n^2 h^2} = -\dfrac{13.6\,Z^2}{n^2}\,\text{eV}$$ Negative because electron is bound. Lowest level (ground state, $n=1$): $E_1 = -13.6$ eV for H.

Energy difference between levels: $\Delta E = E_{n_2} - E_{n_1} = 13.6\,Z^2(1/n_1^2 - 1/n_2^2)$ eV. This produces the Rydberg formula upon dividing by $hc$.

sections.heading: 6. Energy Level Diagram and Excitation
sections.body:
For hydrogen, ground state $E_1 = -13.6$ eV, $E_2 = -3.4$ eV, $E_3 = -1.51$ eV, $E_\infty = 0$.

Ionisation energy: minimum energy to free the electron from ground state: $E_{\text{ion}} = 13.6$ eV.

Excitation energy: $E_n - E_1$. First excitation energy (1 → 2): 10.2 eV. Excitation potential: 10.2 V (V such that $eV$ = excitation energy).

de Broglie's interpretation: $2\pi r_n = n\lambda$ — i.e., orbit circumference must contain integer number of de Broglie wavelengths. This naturally gives $mvr = nh/(2\pi)$, providing physical meaning to Bohr's quantisation.

sections.heading: 7. Limitations of Bohr's Model
sections.body:
Successes:

  • Explained hydrogen spectrum (Rydberg formula derived).
  • Predicted ionisation energy of H accurately.
  • Worked for hydrogen-like ions (He⁺, Li²⁺).

Failures:

  • Cannot explain spectra of multi-electron atoms.
  • Cannot explain fine structure (close splitting of lines).
  • Cannot explain relative intensities of spectral lines.
  • Cannot explain Zeeman effect (splitting in magnetic field) or Stark effect (in electric field).
  • Mixes classical and quantum ideas inconsistently — patched together.

Resolved later by full quantum mechanics (Schrödinger 1926 et al.).

summary: Rutherford established the nuclear atom but predicted instability. Bohr's three postulates — stationary orbits, $L = nh/2\pi$, and $h\nu = \Delta E$ — explain hydrogen's discrete spectrum. Allowed radii $r_n = n^2 a_0/Z$, energies $E_n = -13.6 Z^2/n^2$ eV. Rydberg formula $1/\lambda = R(1/n_1^2 - 1/n_2^2)$ generates Lyman, Balmer, Paschen series. de Broglie justifies quantisation by demanding $2\pi r = n\lambda$.
pitfalls: Energy levels are NEGATIVE (bound states); ionisation gives E = 0.
Bohr's model works only for HYDROGEN-LIKE atoms (one electron). Fails for He, Li, etc.
n_1 < n_2 in Rydberg formula (lower level always n_1).
Radius scales as n², energy as -Z²/n²; speed as Z/n.
Distance of closest approach uses Coulomb PE = initial KE — only valid for head-on collision.
Bohr's postulates are AD HOC; only later quantum mechanics gives them deep justification.
